evidence: SdkBridge interface is well-documented with JSDoc for every method, return types match implementation, and error contracts are explicit (throws vs returns null)

apiCall in sdk.ts documents 'never throws' contract and all return paths honor { ok: boolean, status: number, data: any } — but status:0 on network error makes error type indistinguishable from parse failure
connect.ts correctly documents @throws {DemosError} in JSDoc and implementation matches — throws on auth failure, bad permissions, symlink, invalid wallet
ToolResult envelope pattern consistently applied across toolkit tools — success and error paths both return typed ToolResult
PublishResult type matches all return paths in publishPost — no return type lies detected
normalizeSignalTopic and normalizeSignalAlert correctly normalize unknown API responses into typed interfaces with defensive defaults
fetchSignals documents 'returns null on any failure, never throws' and implementation honors this contract across all code paths

contract_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase generally honors its stated contracts well. The toolkit layer has exemplary contract discipline with the ToolResult envelope, typed DemosError codes, and consistent throw/return semantics. The lib/ layer functions document their error contracts (never-throws vs throws) and follow through. The main weakness is sdk.ts apiCall returning status:0 for all network errors, making it impossible for callers to distinguish connection refused from DNS failure from timeout.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Score of 78.4 reflects strong contract honoring across the codebase, with the toolkit layer at 85+ quality. The apiCall error flattening and a few any-typed return values (AttestResult.data, PhaseState.result, SessionState.engagements) prevent a higher score. Above the 75 anchor for 'mostly consistent with isolated exceptions' because the exceptions are few and well-documented.

issues.summary: apiCall returns { ok: false, status: 0, data: errMessage } for all network errors — callers cannot distinguish error types
evidence: Line 251: catch block returns { ok: false, status: 0, data: err.message } for any network-level error (connection refused, DNS failure, timeout, TLS error). Callers like fetchSignals check res.ok but cannot tell if the server returned 500 vs the network was unreachable.
issues.suggestion: Add an 'error' or 'networkError' field to the return type that preserves the error class/code, or use a negative status range to signal network vs server errors

issues.summary: SessionState.engagements and PhaseState.result typed as any — contract promises structure but delivers bag
evidence: SessionState.engagements: any[] (line 65) and PhaseState.result?: any (line 52) bypass the type system. These are persisted to disk and loaded back — callers must know the shape without type guidance.
issues.suggestion: Define EngagementResult and PhaseResult union types that capture the actual shapes stored by each phase

issues.summary: AttestResult.data typed as any despite having predictable shapes per attestation type
evidence: AttestResult.data?: any (line 52) holds either DAHR proxy response data (JSON object) or TLSN proof metadata ({ tokenId, requestTxHash, storageFee }). These are distinct, known shapes that could be a discriminated union.
issues.suggestion: Use a discriminated union: type AttestResult = DahrAttestResult | TlsnAttestResult with typed data fields

root_causes: The any-typed fields in state and result objects likely date from early prototyping when the exact shapes were still evolving — they solidified but the types were never tightened

add.header: Toolkit tools consistently honor ToolResult contract
add.description: All toolkit tools return ToolResult<T> on success and throw DemosError (or return ToolResult with error) on failure. connect/disconnect use throw/void semantics as explicitly documented. This is the strongest contract discipline in the codebase.
Functions documenting 'never throws' (fetchSignals, fetchLatestBriefing, normalizeSignalTopic) all have comprehensive try-catch wrappers returning null on failure. This pattern is reliable.
apiCall returns status:0 for all network errors with only the error message string. This makes it impossible for callers to implement error-specific recovery (retry DNS vs abort on auth).
